subject: [DCEAGEN] Add app-config volume mount to common dceagen2 template
commit_message:
[DCEAGEN] Add app-config volume mount to common dceagen2 template

Mount volume containing app configuration to all deployments in dcaegen2

Signed-off-by: Bartosz Gardziejewski <bartosz.gardziejewski@nokia.com>
Issue-ID: DCAEGEN2-2692
Change-Id: I414c44633f7dd1b0a37b135e5cbc2f2111494a0b

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/oom-master-commit-lint/1825/ : FAILURE

It seems that you don"t have a commit body.

You must add at least one sentence explaining the purpose of the commit ( https://jenkins.onap.org/job/oom-master-commit-lint/1825/ )

The Commit title must follow this pattern "[WHAT] A title"

WHAT can be a component name (AAI for example), DOC for documentation, COMMON for common, ...

The title must start with a capital letter (A in the example) and a space must be set between the end of the topic and the actual title: "] A title" ( https://jenkins.onap.org/job/oom-master-commit-lint/1825/ )

Patch Set 4:

Would suggest following order for review/merge on template updates
https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120618
https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120211
and then this patch (https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120756)

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> Would suggest following order for review/merge on template updates
> https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120618
> https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120211
> and then this patch (https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120756)

Vijay (Perhaps Lucas as well):
I think, we shall be fine to preserve such an order as You propose.
However, the team noticed something like this for "envsubst" container:
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /config-input
          name: app-config-input
        - mountPath: /config
          name: app-config
        image: {{ include "repositoryGenerator.image.envsubst}}

The team noticed, that there is an operation done on the app-config-input configMap --> The contents are processed as ENV variables and then stored to app-config volume; then this volume is exposed to other containers.

I would assume, that we`d like to expose to the DCAE collectors the configMap (so app-config-input) itself, and not really the processed files, stored then in a app-config volume.

Could You please confirm this?

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> > Patch Set 4:
> > 
> > Would suggest following order for review/merge on template updates
> > https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120618
> > https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120211
> > and then this patch (https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120756)
> 
> Vijay (Perhaps Lucas as well):
> I think, we shall be fine to preserve such an order as You propose.
> However, the team noticed something like this for "envsubst" container:
>         volumeMounts:
>         - mountPath: /config-input
>           name: app-config-input
>         - mountPath: /config
>           name: app-config
>         image: {{ include "repositoryGenerator.image.envsubst}}
> 
> The team noticed, that there is an operation done on the app-config-input configMap --> The contents are processed as ENV variables and then stored to app-config volume; then this volume is exposed to other containers.
> 
> I would assume, that we`d like to expose to the DCAE collectors the configMap (so app-config-input) itself, and not really the processed files, stored then in a app-config volume.
> 
> Could You please confirm this?

I think you want the processed files.  The environment variable substitution is used to replace placeholder values for credentials with the actual values from a secret.

The configuration that's pushed into Consul is the processed file, not the original configMap.

Patch Set 4:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> > Patch Set 4:
> > 
> > > Patch Set 4:
> > > 
> > > Would suggest following order for review/merge on template updates
> > > https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120618
> > > https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120211
> > > and then this patch (https://gerrit.onap.org/r/c/oom/+/120756)
> > 
> > Vijay (Perhaps Lucas as well):
> > I think, we shall be fine to preserve such an order as You propose.
> > However, the team noticed something like this for "envsubst" container:
> >         volumeMounts:
> >         - mountPath: /config-input
> >           name: app-config-input
> >         - mountPath: /config
> >           name: app-config
> >         image: {{ include "repositoryGenerator.image.envsubst}}
> > 
> > The team noticed, that there is an operation done on the app-config-input configMap --> The contents are processed as ENV variables and then stored to app-config volume; then this volume is exposed to other containers.
> > 
> > I would assume, that we`d like to expose to the DCAE collectors the configMap (so app-config-input) itself, and not really the processed files, stored then in a app-config volume.
> > 
> > Could You please confirm this?
> 
> I think you want the processed files.  The environment variable substitution is used to replace placeholder values for credentials with the actual values from a secret.
> 
> The configuration that's pushed into Consul is the processed file, not the original configMap.

Ok, understood, but...
This will mean, we cannot support the live configuration update, as the changes done in the configMap will not be reflected in the processed file (as far as I see, envsubst is an initContainer, not a sidecar container).

Your view? Vijay?
Would it be fine to start with this approach, that we start with configuration load at start-up?
Or shall we think of sth like using secret names in the configMap, so we can resolve them in the CBS-Client?

> 
> Your view? Vijay?
> Would it be fine to start with this approach, that we start with configuration load at start-up?
> Or shall we think of sth like using secret names in the configMap, so we can resolve them in the CBS-Client?

I believe there's a "best practice" recommendation somewhere (I can't find the reference) for applications to store credentials separately from other configuration information--in a separate file perhaps, or in environment variables.   If we were to do that, then we would not need to do the envsubst on the non-credential configuration, and we could mount the configMap with the non-credential configuration directly on the container.  This requires a change to the applications, so that they no longer looks for credentials in the same place as the other configuration.  Potentially this could all be handled in the CBS client, so that the main application code doesn't need to change.

Patch Set 5:

Gitlab Orange 4: Chained-CI build started (pipeline id: 291338684), max time: 14400 seconds

Patch Set 5:

> Patch Set 5:
> 
> > > The configuration that's pushed into Consul is the processed file, not the original configMap.
> > 
> > Ok, understood, but...
> > This will mean, we cannot support the live configuration update, as the changes done in the configMap will not be reflected in the processed file (as far as I see, envsubst is an initContainer, not a sidecar container).
> > 
> > Your view? Vijay?
> > Would it be fine to start with this approach, that we start with configuration load at start-up?
> > Or shall we think of sth like using secret names in the configMap, so we can resolve them in the CBS-Client?
> 
> I believe there's a "best practice" recommendation somewhere (I can't find the reference) for applications to store credentials separately from other configuration information--in a separate file perhaps, or in environment variables.   If we were to do that, then we would not need to do the envsubst on the non-credential configuration, and we could mount the configMap with the non-credential configuration directly on the container.  This requires a change to the applications, so that they no longer looks for credentials in the same place as the other configuration.  Potentially this could all be handled in the CBS client, so that the main application code doesn't need to change.

Except for DMAAP secure configuration (which includes password hence secret are better), don’t see secret necessary for other app-configuration (including dmaap static unauthenticated topics).

Regarding handling dynamic config updates – this is feasible by using either config-map or file mounted. The configmap “x-application-config-configmap" is used for initial load, however when data is updated, this get reflected within the app container ( need “app-config-input” included as volumemount). So for any updates on configmap post deploy, CBS SDK can use either configuration directly from application-config-configmap or based on file mounted (app-config-input path).  Though this may result in data inconsistency between what is loaded in consul (at startup) and config-map/file itself - dont see it as an issue as only one can be active/primary source at any given time. This can be determined based on SDK version. For components relying on CONSUL first can continue to stay on current SDK library version; services relying on configmap will switch to new SDK client.   Pls let me know if this is clear or if I’m overlooking some aspect.

One possible complication for SDK could for handling DMAAP secure configuration – this will be available at initial deployment, no change is expected. However the design questions remain if this will be included on x-application-config-configmap or as secret (in which case CBS SDK client will have to look into another file – similar to policy configuration).  Any preference/suggestion – do let me know.

The configmap “x-application-config-configmap" is used for initial load, however when data is updated, this get reflected within the app container ( need “app-config-input” included as volumemount). So for any updates on configmap post deploy, CBS SDK can use either configuration directly from application-config-configmap or based on file mounted (app-config-input path). We have decide to use configuration loaded by envsubst container (app-config) for now. 
Nonetheless we are planning to refactor dceagen2 containers in order to remove envsubst container in the future. 
We will implement architecture in which app-config is loaded directly from configmap and secrets are loaded from environment inside of main container. Mapping of secrets values from environments, most probably, would be done in CBS-client

The configmap “x-application-config-configmap" is used for initial load, however when data is updated, this get reflected within the app container ( need “app-config-input” included as volumemount). So for any updates on configmap post deploy, CBS SDK can use either configuration directly from application-config-configmap or based on file mounted (app-config-input path).  Though this may result in data inconsistency between what is loaded in consul (at startup) and config-map/file itself - dont see it as an issue as only one can be active/primary source at any given time. > 
> We have decide to use configuration loaded by envsubst container (app-config) for now. 
> Nonetheless we are planning to refactor dceagen2 containers in order to remove envsubst container in the future. 
> We will implement architecture in which app-config is loaded directly from configmap and secrets are loaded from environment inside of main container. Mapping of secrets values from environments, most probably, would be done in CBS-client

This will need some further discussion. 

From operation standpoint (as consul will be out of picture soon), need to maintain configfile (mounted into the container) to know active configurations used by MS. This file should be current always and include updates made to configmap and secrets. 
 
The change to CBS clients will have to be assessed also from python lib hence would suggest not adding too much complexity to be handled within library (either java/python). If substitution logic can be handled generic in template and written into file, that will simplify library implementation.

Considering future usecase - inaddition to current config, policy config will also be written into file (and possibly dynamic dmaap config) which libraries may need to include part of CBS query. Keeping source as file(s) may be consistent approach for SDK/library.

Also with the new design, need to minimize the app container part of this migration if possible.
